concepts.title: Motion in a vertical Circle
concepts.conceptBody:
Motion in a Vertical Circle

Suppose a particle of mass $\text{m}$ is attached to an inextensible light string of length $\text{R}$. The particle is moving in a vertical circle of radius $\text{R}$ about a fixed point $\text{O}$. It is imparted a velocity $\text{u}$ in horizontal direction at lowest point $\text{A}$. Let $\text{v}$ be is velocity at point $\text{B}$ of the circle as shown in figure. Here

$\text{h}=\text{R}\left( 1-\text{cos}\theta \right)$ …(i)

From conservation of mechanical energy.

$\frac{1}{2}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m}\left( {{\text{u}}^{2}}-{{\text{v}}^{2}} \right)=\text{mgh}$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{v}}^{2}}={{\text{u}}^{2}}-2\text{gh}$ …(ii)

The necessary centripetal force is provided by the resultant of tension $\text{T}$ and $\text{mgcos}\theta $

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ T}-\text{mgcos}\theta =\frac{\text{m}{{\text{v}}^{2}}}{\text{R}}$ …(iii)

concepts.title: Condition to complete full circle
concepts.conceptBody:
Condition of Looping the Loop

The particle will complete the circle if the string does not slack even at the highest point $\left( \theta =\pi \right)$. Thus, tension in the string should be greater than or equal to zero ( $\text{T}\ge 0)$ at $\theta =\pi $. In critical case substituting $\text{T}=0$ and $\theta =\pi $ in Eq. (iii), we get

\(\mathrm{mg}=\frac{\mathrm{mv}_{\min }^2}{\mathrm{R}}\)

Or $\text{v}_{\text{min}}^{2}=\text{gR}$

Or ${{\text{v}}_{\text{min}}}=\sqrt{\text{gR}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$(at highest point)

Substituting $\theta =\pi $ in Eq. (i), $\text{h}=2\text{R}$

Therefore, from Eq. (ii) $\text{u}_{\text{min }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}^{2}=\text{v}_{\text{min }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}^{2}+2\text{gh}$

Or $\text{u}_{\text{min }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}^{2}=\text{gR}+2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ g}\left( 2\text{R} \right)=5\text{gR}$

or $\text{u}_{\text{min }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}^{2}=\sqrt{5\text{gR}}$

 

Thus, if $u\ge \sqrt{5\text{gR}}$, the particle will complete the circle. At $\text{u}$ $\ge \sqrt{5\text{gR}}$, velocity at highest point is $\text{v}=\sqrt{\text{gR}}$ and tension in the string is zero.

Substituting $\theta =0$ and $v=\sqrt{5\text{gR}}$ in Eq. (iii), we get $\text{T}=6\text{mg}$ or in the critical condition tension in the string at lowest position is $6\text{mg}$. This is shown in figure.

concepts.title: Condition of leaving the circle
concepts.conceptBody:
Condition of Leaving the Circle $(\sqrt{2gR}<u<\sqrt{5gR})$ 

If $\text{u}<\sqrt{5\text{gR}}$, the tension in the string will become zero before reaching the highest point. From Eq. (iii), tension in the string becomes zero $\left( \text{T}=0 \right)$.

where, $\text{cos}\theta =\frac{-{{\text{v}}^{2}}}{\text{Rg}}$

or $\text{cos}\theta =\frac{2\text{gh}-{{\text{u}}^{2}}}{\text{Rg}}$

Substituting, this value of $\text{cos}\theta $ in Eq. (i), we get

$\begin{matrix} \frac{2gh-{{u}^{2}}}{Rg}=1-\frac{h}{R} \\ \text{ or }h=\frac{{{u}^{2}}+Rg}{3g}={{h}_{1}}\text{ (say) }...\text{(iv) } \\ \end{matrix}$

or we can say that at height ${{h}_{1}}$ tension in the string becomes zero. Further, if $u<\sqrt{5\text{ gR }}$, velocity of the particle becomes zero when or

$\begin{matrix} 0={{\text{u}}^{2}}-2\text{gh} \\ \text{or } \text{h}=\frac{{{\text{u}}^{2}}}{2\text{ g}}={{\text{h}}_{2}}\text{ (say) } \\ \end{matrix}$

i.e., at height ${{\text{h}}_{2}}$ velocity of particle becomes zero.

Now, the particle will leave the circle if tension in the string becomes zero but velocity is not zero. or $\text{T}=0$ but $\text{v}\ne 0$. This is possible only when

$\begin{matrix} {} & {{\text{h}}_{1}}<{{\text{h}}_{2}} \\ \text{ or } & \frac{{{\text{u}}^{2}}+\text{Rg}}{3\text{ g}}<\frac{{{\text{u}}^{2}}}{2\text{ g}} \\ \text{ or } & 2{{\text{u}}^{2}}+2\text{Rg}<3{{\text{u}}^{2}} \\ \text{ or } & {{\text{u}}^{2}}>2\text{Rg} \\ \text{ or } & \text{u}>\sqrt{2\text{Rg}} \\ \end{matrix}$

 

Therefore, if $\sqrt{2gR}<u<\sqrt{5gR}$, the particle leaves the circle.

From Eq. (iv), we can see that $h>R$ if ${{u}^{2}}>2gR$. Thus, the particle, will leave the circle when $\text{h}>\text{R}$ or $90<\theta <180$. This situation is shown in the figure.

$\text{ or }\sqrt{2\text{gR}}<\text{u}<\sqrt{5\text{gR}}\text{ or }90<\theta <180$

Note: That after leaving the circle, the particle will follow a parabolic path.

concepts.title: Condition of oscillation
concepts.conceptBody:
Condition of Oscillation $(0<u<\sqrt{2gR})$ 

The particle will oscillate if velocity of the particle becomes zero but tension in the string is not zero. or $\text{v}=0$, but $\text{T}\ne 0$. This is possible when

$\begin{matrix} & {{h}_{2}}<{{h}_{1}} & \\ \text{ or } & \frac{{{u}^{2}}}{2g}<\frac{{{u}^{2}}+Rg}{3g} & \text{ or }3{{u}^{2}}<2{{u}^{2}}+2Rg \\ \text{ or } & {{u}^{2}}<2Rg & \text{ or }u<\sqrt{2Rg} \\ \end{matrix}$

Moreover, if ${{\text{h}}_{1}}={{\text{h}}_{2}},\text{u}=\sqrt{2\text{Rg}}$ and tension and velocity both becomes zero simultaneously.

 

Further, from Eq. (iv), we can see that $h\le \text{R}$ if . Thus, for $0<\text{u}<\sqrt{2\text{Rg}}$, particle oscillates in lower half of the circle $(0<\theta \le 90)$. This situation is shown in the figure.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & 0<\text{u}\le \sqrt{2\text{Rg}} \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & 0<\theta \le 90 \\ \end{array}$

Note: The above three conditions have been derived for a particle moving in a vertical circle attached to a string. The same conditions apply if a particle moves inside a smooth spherical shell of radius $R$. The only difference is that the tension is replaced by the normal reaction $\text{N}$.

concepts.title: Motion of a ball over a smooth solid sphere
concepts.conceptBody:
Motion of a ball over a Smooth Solid Sphere

Suppose a small ball of mass $\text{m}$ is given a velocity $\text{v}$ over the top of a smooth sphere of radius $\text{R}$. The equation of motion for the ball at the topmost point will be.

 

$\text{mg}-\text{N}=\frac{\text{m}{{\text{v}}^{2}}}{\text{R}}$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ N}=\text{mg}-\frac{\text{m}{{\text{v}}^{2}}}{\text{R}}$

From this equation we see that value of $\text{N}$ decreases as $\text{v}$ increases. Minimum value of $\text{N}$ can be zero Hence,

$0=\text{mg}-\frac{\text{mv}_{\text{max}}^{2}}{\text{R}}$

Or ${{\text{v}}_{\text{max}}}=\sqrt{\text{Rg}}$

From here we can conclude that ball will lose contact with the sphere right from the beginning velocity of ball at topmost point $\text{v}>\sqrt{\text{Rg}}$. If $\text{v}<\sqrt{\text{Rg}}$ it will definitely lose contact but after moving certain distance over the sphere. Now let us find the angle $\theta $ where the ball loses contact with the sphere velocity at topmost point is just zero. Fig. (b)

$\begin{matrix} \text{h}=\text{R}\left( 1-\text{cos}\theta \right)...(i) \\ {{\text{v}}^{2}}=2\text{gh }...\text{(ii)} \\ \text{mgcos}\theta =\frac{\text{m}{{\text{v}}^{2}}}{\text{R}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ as }\!\!~\!\!\text{ N}=0 \right)...(iii) \\ \end{matrix}$

Solving Eqs. (i), (ii) and (iii), we get

$\theta =\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{-1}}\left( \frac{2}{3} \right)={{48.2}^{\circ }}$

Thus the ball can move on the sphere maximum upto $\theta =\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{-1}}\left( \frac{2}{3} \right)$.

examples.title: A stone is executing vertical circular motion with the help of a string as shown below. The string will have least tension at point.

A particle is whirled in a vertical circle of radius $1.0 \mathrm{~m}$ using a string with one end fixed. If the ratio of maximum to minimum tensions in the string is $5 / 3$, then the minimum velocity (in $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$ ) of the particle during circular motion is
A fighter plane is moving in a vertical circle of radius ' $r$ '. Its minimum velocity at the highest point of the circle will be

A stone of mass $1 \mathrm{~kg}$ is tied with a string and it is whirled in a vertical circle of radius $1 \mathrm{~m}$. If tension at the highest point is $14 \mathrm{~N}$, then velocity at lowest point will be closest to:

A frictionless track $A B C D E$ ends in a circular loop of radius $R$. A body slides down the track from point $A$ which is at $a$ height $h=5 \mathrm{~cm}$. Maximum value of $R$ for the body to successfully complete the loop is
With what minimum speed $v$ must a small ball should be pushed inside a smooth vertical tube from a height $\mathrm{h}$ so that it may reach the top of the tube? Radius of the tube is $R$.

A particle of mass $10 \mathrm{~g}$ moves along a circle of radius $6.4 \mathrm{~cm}$ with a constant tangential acceleration. What is the magnitude of this acceleration if the kinetic energy of the particle becomes equal to $8 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~J}$ boy the end of the second revolution after the beginning of the motion?

A body initially at rest and sliding along a frictionless track from a height $\mathrm{h}$ (as shown in the figure) just completes a vertical circle of diameter $\mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{D}$. 'The height $h$ is equal to.

A smooth sphere of radius $\mathrm{R}$ is made to translate line with a constant acceleration $\mathrm{a}=\mathrm{g}$. A particle kept on the top of the sphere is released from there at zero velocity with respect to the sphere. Find the speed of the particle with respect to the sphere as a function of angle $\theta$ as it slides down.
